content:
# Exodus Chapter 6 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~34r–34v (PDF pp. 66–67); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 46–47 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_006_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_006_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** Then YHWH said to Moses, "Now you will see what I will do to Pharaoh. A mighty hand will force him — he will let them go. A mighty hand will force him — he will drive them out of his land."

**2** God spoke to Moses and said, "I am YHWH.

**3** I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob as El Shaddai — but by my name YHWH I was not known to them.

**4** I also established my covenant with them, to give them the land of Canaan — the land where they lived as foreigners.

**5** And now I have heard the groaning of the Israelites, whom the Egyptians are enslaving, and I have remembered my covenant.

**6** "Therefore, say to the Israelites: I am YHWH.
I will bring you out from under the crushing weight of Egypt.
I will rescue you from their slavery.
I will redeem you with an outstretched arm and with mighty acts of judgment.

**7** I will take you as my own people, and I will be your God — and you will know that I am YHWH your God, the one who brought you out from under the crushing weight of Egypt.

**8** I will bring you into the land I swore with uplifted hand to give to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob. I will give it to you as a possession. I am YHWH."

**9** Moses told all of this to the Israelites, but they could not hear him — their spirits were crushed and their slavery too cruel.

**10** Then YHWH said to Moses,

**11** "Go. Tell Pharaoh, king of Egypt, to let the Israelites go from his land."

**12** But Moses said to YHWH, "If the Israelites will not even listen to me, why would Pharaoh? I am no one's idea of a speaker."

**13** Then YHWH spoke to both Moses and Aaron and gave them a charge concerning the Israelites and concerning Pharaoh, king of Egypt — to bring the Israelites out of the land of Egypt.

**14** These are the heads of their ancestral houses:

The sons of Reuben, Israel's firstborn: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi. These are the clans of Reuben.

**15** The sons of Simeon: Jemuel, Jamin, Ohad, Jachin, Zohar, and Shaul the son of a Canaanite woman. These are the clans of Simeon.

**16** These are the names of the sons of Levi, by their generations: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. Levi lived one hundred and thirty-seven years.

**17** The sons of Gershon: Libni and Shimei, by their clans.

**18** The sons of Kohath: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel. Kohath lived one hundred and thirty-three years.

**19** The sons of Merari: Mahli and Mushi. These are the clans of Levi, by their generations.

**20** Amram married his father's sister Jochebed, and she bore him Aaron and Moses. Amram lived one hundred and thirty-seven years.

**21** The sons of Izhar: Korah, Nepheg, and Zichri.

**22** The sons of Uzziel: Mishael, Elzaphan, and Sithri.

**23** Aaron married Elisheba, daughter of Amminadab and sister of Nahshon, and she bore him Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.

**24** The sons of Korah: Assir, Elkanah, and Abiasaph. These are the clans of the Korahites.

**25** Aaron's son Eleazar married one of the daughters of Putiel, and she bore him Phinehas. These are the heads of the ancestral houses of the Levites, by their clans.

**26** It was this Aaron and this Moses to whom YHWH said, "Bring the Israelites out of the land of Egypt, company by company."

**27** It was they who spoke to Pharaoh, king of Egypt, about bringing the Israelites out of Egypt — this same Moses and Aaron.

**28** On the day YHWH spoke to Moses in the land of Egypt,

**29** YHWH said to him, "I am YHWH. Tell Pharaoh, king of Egypt, everything I say to you."

**30** But Moses said to YHWH, "I am no one's idea of a speaker — why would Pharaoh listen to me?"

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v.1 — "A mighty hand will force him" (Heb. *beyad chazaqah*, בְּיָ֣ד חֲזָקָ֔ה):** YHWH's answer to Moses' raw complaint at the end of chapter 5 is not an explanation but a promise of overwhelming action. The Hebrew uses *beyad chazaqah* twice — "by a strong hand" — first for letting them go (*yeshallachem*) and then for driving them out (*yegareshem*). The word *garash* is violent — to expel, to drive out, the way one drives out an enemy. Pharaoh will not merely permit their departure; he will be desperate to see them leave. The Vaticanus reads *en cheiri krataia* (ἐν χειρὶ κραταιᾷ), "with a mighty hand," and adds *meta brachionos hupsēlou* (μετὰ βραχίονος ὑψηλοῦ), "with an uplifted arm." PNEUMA repeats the phrase to mirror the Hebrew doubling, emphasizing the irresistible force of what is coming.

**v.3 — "as El Shaddai" (Heb. *be'El Shaddai*, בְּאֵ֣ל שַׁדָּ֑י):** This verse marks one of the most significant theological moments in the Torah. God distinguishes between how the ancestors knew him — as *El Shaddai* (traditionally "God Almighty," though the etymology is debated; possibly "God of the Mountain" or "God Who Is Sufficient") — and his name *YHWH*, which he now claims was not *known* to them. The Hebrew *lo noda'ti lahem* (לֹ֥א נוֹדַ֖עְתִּי לָהֶֽם) uses the Niphal of *yada* — "I was not made known." This is not about the name's existence (Genesis uses YHWH freely) but about its experiential meaning. The patriarchs knew God as the provider of promises; this generation will know God as the one who *acts* — who breaks empires and keeps covenants by force. The name YHWH, linked to the verb *hayah* ("to be"), will be filled with content through the events of the Exodus. The Vaticanus renders *El Shaddai* as *theos on autōn* (θεὸς ὢν αὐτῶν), "being their God," losing the specific divine title but preserving the relational dynamic.

**vv.6-8 — The four "I will" declarations (Heb. four verbs with *vehotse'ti... vehitsalti... vega'alti... velaqachti*, וְהוֹצֵאתִי... וְהִצַּלְתִּי... וְגָאַלְתִּי... וְלָקַחְתִּי):** These four promises form the structural backbone of the passage and are the basis for the four cups of wine at the Passover Seder. Each verb escalates: (1) *hotse'ti* — "I will bring out" (physical extraction); (2) *hitsalti* — "I will rescue" (deliverance from the power itself); (3) *ga'alti* — "I will redeem" (*ga'al* is the kinsman-redeemer verb — the language of a family member buying back a relative from debt-slavery, profoundly intimate); (4) *laqachti* — "I will take" (the language of covenant marriage — "I will take you as my people"). The movement is from external liberation to covenantal union. Some traditions identify a fifth promise in v.8 — *veheve'ti*, "I will bring in" — corresponding to a fifth cup at Seder (Elijah's cup, left for the future). PNEUMA preserves the escalation by rendering each verb with distinct English force. The phrase "crushing weight" for *sivlot Mitsrayim* (סִבְלֹ֣ת מִצְרַ֔יִם) renders the Hebrew *sivlot* ("burden-bearing"), the same word used in 1:11 and 5:4-5, maintaining the narrative thread of Egypt's specific cruelty. The Vaticanus uses *katadunasteia* (καταδυναστεία), "oppression/tyranny."

**v.9 — "their spirits were crushed and their slavery too cruel" (Heb. *miqqotser ruach ume'avodah qashah*, מִקֹּ֣צֶר ר֔וּחַ וּמֵעֲבֹדָ֖ה קָשָֽׁה):** The Hebrew *qotser ruach* is devastating — literally "shortness of spirit/breath." It describes people so beaten down that they cannot even draw a deep enough breath to hope. The noun *qotser* comes from *qatsar* ("to be short, to cut short"); the spirit has been abbreviated, truncated by suffering. Combined with *avodah qashah* ("hard/cruel labor"), it paints a picture of total human depletion — body and soul. God's grandest covenant speech falls on ears that pain has sealed shut. This is not stubbornness or unbelief; it is the spiritual damage that sustained oppression inflicts. The people have been so thoroughly crushed that even good news cannot reach them. The Vaticanus uses *oligopsychia* (ὀλιγοψυχία) — "smallness of soul" — and *erga sklēra* (ἔργα σκληρά), "harsh works."

**v.12 — "I am no one's idea of a speaker" (Heb. *aral sefatayim*, עֲרַ֥ל שְׂפָתָ֖יִם):** Literally "uncircumcised of lips." The image is startling: *aral* (ערל) means "uncircumcised," implying something blocked, sealed, unfit. Moses describes his mouth the way one would describe a covenant-less body — as if his lips have a foreskin that prevents speech from emerging properly. The metaphor connects speech to covenant fitness and reappears in v.30. It is more extreme than the "heavy mouth" of 4:10; here Moses frames his inadequacy in the language of sacred exclusion. The Vaticanus renders it *alogos* (ἄλογος), "without speech/reason," flattening the vivid Hebrew image. PNEUMA uses "no one's idea of a speaker" to capture the self-deprecating force without literalizing the circumcision metaphor, which would distract an English reader from the emotional weight.

**vv.14-25 — The genealogy:** This family record appears to interrupt the narrative at an odd moment — God has just commissioned Moses, and Moses has just protested his inadequacy. But the genealogy is not accidental. It establishes the tribal and priestly credentials of Moses and Aaron precisely at the point where their authority must be grounded. It begins with Reuben and Simeon (the first two sons of Jacob) not because they are relevant but because the list must reach Levi — the third son — in proper birth order. Once it arrives at Levi, it expands in extraordinary detail: three generations (Kohath, Amram, Moses/Aaron) with lifespans, marriages, and clan structures. The purpose is to say: *these are the men*. They are not nobodies. They come from a specific line, a documented family, a known ancestry. In a culture where lineage is authority, the genealogy is a credential. Note that v.20 records Amram's marriage to Jochebed (*Yokheved*), his father's sister (*dodat aviv*, דּוֹדָתוֹ֙) — a union later prohibited in Leviticus 18:12. The text records this without comment.

**vv.26-27 — "It was this Aaron and this Moses... this same Moses and Aaron":** The narrator pauses to anchor the genealogy back to the story with an emphatic identification. The reversal of name order — "Aaron and Moses" then "Moses and Aaron" — is deliberate. In the genealogical context, Aaron appears first (he is the older brother). In the narrative context of speaking to Pharaoh, Moses comes first (he is the protagonist). The Hebrew *hu Aharon u-Mosheh... hu Mosheh ve-Aharon* uses the emphatic pronoun *hu* ("he, this one") to say: the same men from this family tree are the same men who stand before Pharaoh. Genealogy meets destiny.

**vv.28-30 — The resumed commission:** The chapter ends by looping back to vv.10-12, repeating the commission and Moses' objection almost verbatim. This resumptive repetition (*Wiederaufnahme*) is a standard Hebrew narrative technique: when a genealogy or other insert interrupts a story, the narrator restarts the scene from just before the interruption. The repetition of *aral sefatayim* in v.30 — "uncircumcised of lips" — drives home that the genealogy has not resolved Moses' self-doubt. His credentials are established, but his confidence is not. The chapter closes on an open question: how will God answer the inadequacy of his own chosen messenger?

— *PNEUMA*
